question:
A random sample of 395 people were surveyed. Each person was asked their interest in riding a bicycle (Variable A) and their age (Variable B). The data that resulted from the survey is summarized in Table 3.10.

Is there evidence to conclude, at the 0.05 level, that the desire to ride a bicycle depends on age? In other words, is age independent of the desire to ride a bicycle?
answer:
Here $r=2$ and $c=4$. The expected number in each cell can be obtained as shown in Table 3.11:

$$
 egin{aligned}
& E_{11}= rac{1}{395}\left(\sum_{i=1}^2 O_{i 1}ight)\left(\sum_{j=1}^4 O_{1 j}ight)= rac{1}{395}(100)(201)=50.8861 \
& E_{12}= rac{1}{395}\left(\sum_{i=1}^2 O_{i 2}ight)\left(\sum_{j=1}^4 O_{1 j}ight)= rac{1}{395}(98)(201)=49.8684 \
& E_{13}= rac{1}{395}(99)(201)=50.3772 \
& \ldots \
& E_{23}= rac{1}{395}(99)(194)=48.6228 \
& E_{24}= rac{1}{395}(98)(194)=48.1316
\end{aligned}
$$


The test statistic value

$$
\lambda_{v a l u e}^2=\sum_{i=1}^2 \sum_{j=1}^4  rac{\left(O_{i j}-E_{i j}ight)^2}{E_{i j}}
$$
$$
 egin{aligned}
& = rac{(60-50.8861)^2}{50.8861}+ rac{(54-49.8684)^2}{49.8684}+\ldots+ rac{(57-48.1316)^2}{48.1316} \
& =8.0062
\end{aligned}
$$


Here $\alpha=0.05$. So $\chi_{0.05,(2-1)(4-1)}^2=\chi_{0.05,3}^2=7.815$. Since $\chi_{	ext {value }}^2=8.0062>$ 7.815 we, therefore, reject the null hypothesis. That is, there is sufficient evidence, at the 0.05 level, to conclude that the desire to ride a bicycle depends on age.
